market_context: The May 8-14 window was defined by three colliding forces. First, a regulatory/legal catalyst: the U.S. Court of International Trade ruled on May 7 that Trump's 10% global tariffs under Section 122 were unlawful, removing a tail risk and lifting market sentiment into the week. Second, a macro shock: April CPI printed 3.8% YoY on May 12 (vs 3.7% consensus), the hottest reading since May 2023, driven by energy (+17.9% YoY, Iran war premium) and shelter re-acceleration. Futures immediately turned negative, rate-hike odds spiked to ~30%, and 10Y yields surged to 4.46%. Markets nevertheless held near ATH, with the S&P 500 briefly touching new highs on May 14. Third, and most dominant: the Trump-Xi summit. Trump flew to Beijing on May 13 with 17 corporate executives including Musk, Tim Cook, and Jensen Huang. The key surprise was the U.S. clearing Nvidia H200 chip sales to ~10 Chinese firms (Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance, JD.com), sending NVDA to a new ATH of $230 on May 14. However, by May 15, it emerged that Beijing was blocking actual purchases and the summit produced 'nothing of real substance' (Fortune headline), triggering a global selloff. The regime was: semis benefited from H200 approval headlines but gave back gains post-summit; China plays (FXI) disappointed; inflation assets (TLT, GLD) underperformed as hot CPI dominated; NVDA's move was front-loaded into the summit date. Exit dates (20 TD from May 8-14) land June 5-11, data unavailable as of May 22 — using May 21 as proxy exit.

trades.thesis: AMD Q1 beat on May 5 (data center +57% YoY, 20+ price target upgrades) signals AI chip cycle intact. Court ruling May 7 removes tariff tail risk. Trump-Xi summit previewed for May 14 with AI chip export controls top of agenda — H200 China sales optionality is a free call option. Semis sector (SOX +65% YTD) has momentum. Buy before summit catalyst.
trades.catalyst: AMD earnings beat validating AI chip demand; CIT tariff ruling reducing macro risk; Trump-Xi summit week AI chip deal speculation
trades.lesson: Correct directional call but sold too early via proxy exit. NVDA ran to $235.74 on May 14 (+9.5% from entry) before giving back post-NVDA-earnings on May 20 (-4% day after results). The trade was a win but max gain required active exit at the May 14 H200 ATH rather than mechanical 20-TD hold.

trades.thesis: Tariff court ruling on May 7 is a regime-clearing event for mega-cap tech. S&P 500 back at ATH. Magnificent Seven earnings outpacing rest of S&P by 40%+. FOMC on hold at 3.5-3.75%, no imminent rate shock. Summit week = tech in focus. Buy broad tech exposure as low-conviction diversifier alongside NVDA.
trades.catalyst: CIT tariff ruling removed legal underpinning for 10% global tariffs; market re-rating tech earnings power
trades.lesson: QQQ essentially flat vs entry over the proxy window — the bull thesis played out intraweek (QQQ hit 719.79 on May 14) but gave back gains when summit disappointed. A better trade structure would have been to take profits on the May 14 ATH rather than holding through the disappointment. The thesis was right, execution window was wrong.

trades.thesis: April CPI printed 3.8% vs 3.7% expected — hottest since May 2023. Energy driving 40%+ of the gain (Iran war premium embedded). Core CPI 0.4% MoM, highest since January 2025. Rate cut expectations evaporate; rate hike odds jump to 30% by year-end per CME data. 10Y yield surged to 4.46%. Long-duration Treasuries face structural headwind. Short TLT is high-conviction macro play.
trades.catalyst: April CPI 3.8% YoY shock; core CPI re-acceleration; rate hike odds spike 30%; 10Y yield hit near 2026 high
trades.lesson: Correct thesis — hot CPI is the clearest macro trade setup of the week. TLT continued declining through May 21. Gain was modest (+0.91%) because bond moves are slow; a leveraged instrument (TBT or put options on TLT) would have amplified the correct directional call. Clean setup, conservative instrument.

trades.thesis: Hot CPI 3.8% paradox trade: gold initially rises on inflation fears but faces competing forces. Iran war premium already embedded in price ($430+). Trump en route to Beijing — geopolitical tension relief possible if summit produces Iran ceasefire progress. China's Xi urged Iran toward diplomacy pre-summit. Risk-on positioning into summit week reduces safe-haven demand. GLD at elevated level relative to real rates given rate hike repricing. Short the crowded safe-haven trade into catalyst resolution.
trades.catalyst: Trump-Xi summit approaching with Iran ceasefire diplomacy potential; risk-on rotation; GLD overbought at $432 with rate hikes repricing against it
trades.lesson: Best trade of the period. GLD fell from $432.93 to $416.99, a clean +3.68% short. The thesis worked for two reasons: (1) risk-on rotation dominated post-CPI as markets held ATH, reducing safe-haven demand; (2) Iran/geopolitical tension priced in began unwinding. The key insight was that a hot CPI in a risk-on environment punishes gold via higher real rates more than it helps via inflation fears.

trades.thesis: Trump lands in Beijing on May 13 with Jensen Huang in the delegation — direct signal that AI chip export controls and H200 sales to China are on the agenda. Reports emerge intraday that U.S. has cleared H200 sales to ~10 Chinese firms including Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance. This restores potentially $3.5-4B in annual China revenue for NVDA. Stock making new ATH. Add on the catalyst confirmation.
trades.catalyst: H200 China export approval rumor confirmed via Reuters; Jensen Huang personally at Trump-Xi summit; Nvidia stock hitting new ATH $225+
trades.lesson: Chased a momentum trade at the top. NVDA peaked at $235.74 on May 14 (day after entry) then retreated — both from the summit disappointment and from the post-earnings selloff pattern (NVDA fell after 3 of last 4 earnings despite beats). Buying on confirmed H200 news was buying the news after the move. The better trade was May 8 entry at $215, not chasing at $225 on confirmation day.

trades.thesis: Trump-Xi summit AI guardrails discussion validates ongoing hyperscaler AI capex. Meta is largest beneficiary of AI-driven ad targeting improvements. Summit week puts AI in global spotlight. Meta Q1 2026 earnings were strong; AI ad monetization accelerating. Risk-on week with market near ATH. Long META as AI infrastructure play with near-term earnings catalyst passed cleanly.
trades.catalyst: Trump-Xi summit AI guardrails agenda validates AI capex; risk-on regime; Meta AI ad revenue momentum
trades.lesson: Summit disappointment weighed on the entire market including META. The thesis was fundamentally sound (Meta AI monetization) but the timing was summit-dependent, and the summit delivered 'nothing of real substance.' META was weak after May 14 — wrong catalyst selection. Should have waited for post-summit clarity before initiating new longs in mega-cap tech.

trades.thesis: Trump returns from China summit with few concrete deals. Boeing deal (200 planes) disappointed vs 500 plane expectations. H200 chip sales approved by U.S. but Beijing itself told Chinese firms NOT to buy — a sovereignty/face-saving move that signals ongoing tensions. Summit produced vague framework, no tariff specifics, no rare earth resolution. Sell the fact on China equities after the anticipatory run. FXI had rallied on summit hopes.
trades.catalyst: Summit 'nothing of real substance' — Fortune headline; Beijing blocking H200 purchases; Boeing deal underwhelmed (200 vs 500 planes expected); global selloff post-summit across Asia indices
trades.lesson: Clean 'sell the news' trade. The summit disappointment was clear by May 14 close — KOSPI lost 6%, CSI 300 -1.12%, Asia broadly down. FXI fell from $37.24 to $35.89 by May 21 (+3.63%). The key signal was that Beijing actively told Chinese companies to reject H200 purchases despite U.S. approval — this revealed that the summit was performative, not substantive.

trades.thesis: PLTR trades at extreme multiples (government/defense AI software). Hot CPI 3.8% is raising rate hike odds to 30% — high-multiple growth stocks are most vulnerable to rate repricing. Summit provided no concrete AI policy clarity. PLTR had run significantly and is government contract dependent; any geopolitical uncertainty or budget scrutiny hurts. Rate hike fear + summit disappointment = multiple compression in speculative high-PE names.
trades.catalyst: CPI hot = rate hike risk (30% odds); summit disappointment removing AI-China optionality speculation; PLTR extended valuation vulnerable to macro repricing
trades.lesson: Wrong on PLTR. Despite rate concerns, PLTR held up and actually rose slightly through May 21 ($137.41 vs entry $133.73). The market treated PLTR as a domestic AI/defense winner insulated from China trade tensions. Rate hike fears (30% odds) were insufficient to break PLTR's momentum — the stock has demonstrated resilience to macro headwinds due to sticky government contracts. Do not short a stock with strong momentum and sticky revenue just on macro concerns.

period_summary.regime_notes: Note: Exit prices use May 21, 2026 (latest available) as proxy for 20-trading-day exits. Actual 20-TD exits fall June 5-11, 2026 (unavailable as of May 22). Results may shift materially. Three dominant regime themes: (1) Tariff/legal uncertainty clearing = buy megacap tech early week — worked. (2) CPI 3.8% shock = short rates/bonds, short gold — TLT short worked; GLD short was best trade of period. (3) Trump-Xi summit = classic buy-the-rumor/sell-the-news on China plays. FXI short on May 14 was correct; chasing NVDA LONG on May 13 at ATH was the key mistake. The week penalized late entrants and rewarded early positioning (NVDA May 8 entry +2.00% vs NVDA May 13 entry -2.80%). TSLA was avoided despite Musk attending summit — correct avoidance as TSLA fell from $445 to $418 by May 21 (-6.1%). Key regime error: initiating new longs into summit catalyst after the move had already happened.
